title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery Queen, Professor Yadley, and Professor Adler discuss whether the crime scenes are connected to the Egyptian Ankh, also known as the Crux Ansata. Elyot suggests that the killer, Croesus, may be deliberately transforming the Ankh—a symbol of life—into a symbol of death by decapitating victims to remove the "head" of the symbol. Professor Yadley refutes this, explaining that the Ankh likely originated from a representation of a shoelace rather than a statue, and noting that its physical form does not inherently imply decapitation.

Ellery subsequently rejects both the Egyptian and American totem pole hypotheses. He argues that if the killer intended to use the Ankh, the victims' heads would have remained intact, and he observes that the T-shaped symbol found near the crime scene lacks specific religious significance in Egypt. He also dismisses the theory of a Christian cross, noting that beheading was not a standard method for executing martyrs. Ellery concludes that the "T" shape may simply be the letter T, though he remains unsettled by the possibility of a coincidence.

The discussion is interrupted by an old woman informing Yadley that a drunk policeman is attempting to break in. Shortly after, Vance arrives with urgent news regarding a telegram from Jamaica. The telegram, signed by Stephen Megalas, states that he has heard of Thomas Braid's death and is traveling to New York immediately.
